planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after water temperatures reach 65°F; position horizontally in submerged containers covered with 6–18 inches of water, with the growing tip pointing upward and just below the soil surface.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in heavy clay or loam soil in a submerged container, positioning the growing tip just below the soil surface under 6–18 inches of warm water in full sun. Rhizomes overwinter safely at the bottom of ponds in zones 5 and above as long as the roots do not freeze solid; in colder zones bring containers indoors or insulate with mulch. Harvest edible rhizomes in autumn after foliage dies back by carefully digging through the submerged mud; seeds ripen inside the distinctive flat-topped seed head through late summer and can be eaten raw, roasted, or dried. Flowers appear June through August on tall stems held well above the water surface.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in saturated or flooded soil after the last frost date, once water temperatures reach at least 60°F; flooding 1–4 inches of standing water over the bed encourages maximum corm production.

directions: Plant corms in heavy, fertile soil kept flooded with 1–4 inches of standing water throughout the growing season, spacing them 12–18 inches apart in full sun. The grass-like green shoots are ornamental but the real reward is underground: each corm sends out horizontal runners that produce a flush of new corms by autumn. Drain the bed or lower water levels in late autumn as foliage yellows, then harvest corms by hand or with a garden fork; freshly harvested water chestnuts have a crisp, sweet flavor far superior to canned. In zones colder than 9, grow as an annual or overwinter corms in a frost-free garage packed in slightly moist sand.

planting.note: Soak tubers overnight before planting 1–2 inches deep in moist to wet soil at or just after the last frost date, when soil temperature reaches at least 65°F; keep soil consistently moist to saturated throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant soaked tubers of the cultivated variety (var. sativus) 1–2 inches deep in loose, moist soil in full sun after the last frost, spacing 6–12 inches apart; this is the domesticated food crop, not the invasive weed form. Keep soil continuously moist to encourage dense networks of underground stolons tipped with nutty, sweet tubers that mature in 90–120 days. Harvest after foliage yellows in early autumn by loosening the soil deeply with a fork and sifting through to collect the small, wrinkled tubers; yield can reach several pounds per plant under ideal conditions. Dried tubers keep for months and are eaten raw as a snack, soaked and blended into the Spanish drink horchata de chufa, or ground into gluten-free flour.

planting.note: Plant corms 4–6 inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in spring once night temperatures remain above 50°F; choose a site with dappled to partial shade as the large single leaf scorches in direct afternoon sun in most climates.

directions: Plant corms in rich, humus-rich soil with reliable moisture and dappled shade, mimicking the forest understory conditions of its native East Asia; each corm produces a single enormous compound leaf on a mottled stalk that can reach 6 feet tall in a single season. Corms grow slowly, increasing in size each year, and are typically harvested after 2–3 years when they can weigh 5–10 pounds; the raw corm contains calcium oxalate crystals and must be processed before eating. Traditional processing involves drying, grinding, and treating the flour with an alkaline solution to form the firm, gelatinous product used in shirataki noodles and konjac blocks. In zones 6–7 mulch the planting site heavily in autumn to protect corms through winter, or dig and store them frost-free.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 3–4 inches deep in moist, well-drained, organically rich soil after temperatures remain above 65°F; this understory plant prefers partial shade and consistent moisture year-round in its tropical range.

directions: Plant rhizome pieces in humid, tropical or subtropical gardens in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil; this Pacific island staple grows naturally in coastal and forest-edge habitats from East Africa to Polynesia. The deeply lobed leaves are attractive, and by 6–9 months the underground rhizomes are large enough to harvest; raw rhizomes are bitter and mildly toxic due to tannins and must be grated, washed repeatedly in water, and dried to yield a pure white starch. The fine arrowroot-like starch, called pia in Hawaii and Polynesia, was a critical famine food and is used to thicken sauces and puddings; it is entirely gluten-free and easily digestible. Outside zones 10–12 grow as a container plant brought indoors for winter, maintaining warmth and humidity.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture and Human Resources
